perturbation.original_text: Each Party shall advise its directors, senior staff, and other employees receiving such Confidential Information of the existence of and the importance of complying with the obligations set forth in this Article.
perturbation.changed_text: Each Party may, at its sole discretion, advise its directors, senior staff, and other employees receiving such Confidential Information of the existence of and the importance of complying with the obligations set forth in this Article.
perturbation.explanation: Changing "shall advise" to "may advise, at its sole discretion" weakens the obligation to inform employees about confidentiality, creating uncertainty about enforcement and potentially violating regulations that require employers to clearly communicate confidentiality obligations.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Economic Espionage Act, 18 U.S. Code В§ 1832
perturbation.law_explanation: The Economic Espionage Act aims to protect trade secrets. By making it discretionary whether to inform employees of confidentiality obligations, the modified text reduces the effort to protect trade secrets, potentially conflicting with the intent of the EEA, which assumes reasonable measures are taken to safeguard proprietary information.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: 18 U.S. Code В§ 1832 - Theft of trade secrets | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! Г— No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 18 U.S. Code В§ 1832 - Theft of trade secrets U.S. Code Notes prev | next (a) Whoever, with intent to convert a trade secret , that is related to a product or service used in or intended for use in interstate or foreign commerce, to the economic benefit of anyone other than the owner thereof, and intending or knowing that the offense will, injure any owner of that trade secret , knowinglyвҖ” (1) steals, or without authorization appropriates, takes, carries away, or conceals, or by fraud, artifice, or deception obtains such information; (2) without authorization copies, duplicates, sketches, draws, photographs, downloads, uploads, alters, destroys, photocopies, replicates, transmits, delivers, sends, mails, communicates, or conveys such information; (3) receives, buys, or possesses such information, knowing the same to have been stolen or appropriated, obtained, or converted without authorization; (4) attempts to commit any offense described in paragraphs (1) through (3); or (5) conspires with one or more other persons to commit any offense described in paragraphs (1) through (3), and one or more of such persons do any act to effect the object of the conspiracy, shall, except as provided in subsection (b), be fined under this title or imprisoned not more than 10 years, or both. (b) Any organization that commits any offense described in subsection (a) shall be fined not more than the greater of $5,000,000 or 3 times the value of the stolen trade secret to the organization, including expenses for research and design and other costs of reproducing the trade secret that the organization has thereby avoided. (Added Pub. L. 104вҖ“294, title I, В§ 101(a) , Oct. 11, 1996 , 110 Stat. 3489 ; amended Pub. L. 112вҖ“236, В§ 2 , Dec. 28, 2012 , 126 Stat. 1627 ; Pub. L. 114вҖ“153, В§ 3(a)(1) , May 11, 2016 , 130 Stat. 382

perturbation.justification: The modification from "shall advise" to "may advise, at its sole discretion" weakens the obligation to inform employees about confidentiality, potentially conflicting with the intent to protect trade secrets under 18 U.S. Code В§ 1832, as indicated in both provided snippets. While the law focuses on the theft of trade secrets, the Law Explanation correctly points out that making employee notification discretionary reduces the effort to protect those secrets, creating a risk of violating the intent of the EEA. By allowing a party to choose whether or not to inform employees, the revised text undermines a reasonable measure expected to safeguard proprietary information, thus presenting an inconsistency and potential legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Following an application to dissolve the Joint Venture Company pursuant to Article 105, the Board of Directors shall forthwith appoint a liquidation committee which shall have the power to represent the Joint Venture Company in all legal matters. The liquidation committee shall value and liquidate Page 25 the Joint Venture Company's assets in accordance with the applicable Chinese laws and regulations and the principles set forth herein.
perturbation.changed_text: Following an application to dissolve the Joint Venture Company pursuant to Article 105, the Board of Directors shall appoint a liquidation committee within 180 days, which shall have the power to represent the Joint Venture Company in all legal matters. The liquidation committee shall value and liquidate Page 25 the Joint Venture Company's assets in accordance with the applicable Chinese laws and regulations and the principles set forth herein.
perturbation.explanation: Delaying the appointment of the liquidation committee by 180 days after an application to dissolve the Joint Venture Company is made could conflict with requirements for expedient liquidation processes.
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 186 of the China Company Law emphasizes that the liquidation committee should exercise its functions and powers pursuant to the law. Delaying the formation of the liquidation committee hinders the prompt commencement of the liquidation process, potentially violating regulations that mandate the timely liquidation of assets and settlement of claims.

perturbation.justification: The original text requires the board of directors to 'forthwith appoint' a liquidation committee, implying immediate action. The changed text allows for a delay of up to 180 days. The Law Explanation states this delay 'hinders the prompt commencement of the liquidation process, potentially violating regulations that mandate the timely liquidation of assets and settlement of claims.' While the scraped snippets do not directly state a time frame, the Law Explanation, based on Article 186 of the China Company Law, indicates that the delay contradicts the legally required timeliness of liquidation processes.

perturbation.original_text: Upon the agreement of all Parties and the unanimous consent of the Board of Directors, an application to extend the Contract Term may be made to the Examination and Approval Authority no less than six (6) months prior to the expiration of the Contract Term.
perturbation.changed_text: Upon the agreement of all Parties and the unanimous consent of the Board of Directors, an application to extend the Contract Term may be made to the Examination and Approval Authority no less than one (1) month prior to the expiration of the Contract Term.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the notification period for contract extension applications from six months to one month may not provide sufficient time for regulatory review and approval, conflicting with administrative requirements.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Regulations on the Implementation of the Law on Chinese-Foreign Equity Joint Ventures, Article 94
perturbation.law_citation: Regulations on the Implementation of the Law on Chinese-Foreign Equity Joint Ventures, Article 94
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 94 of the Regulations on the Implementation of the Law on Chinese-Foreign Equity Joint Ventures states that for an extension of the term of a joint venture, an application shall be submitted to the examination and approval authority six months before the expiration of the term. Changing 'six (6) months' to 'one (1) month' directly contradicts this requirement.
perturbation.justification: The original text states that an application to extend the contract term must be made no less than six months prior to the expiration of the contract term. The changed text alters this period to one month. The law explanation states that Article 94 of the Regulations on the Implementation of the Law on Chinese-Foreign Equity Joint Ventures requires that an application be submitted six months before expiration. The change directly contradicts this established legal timeframe, creating a legal inconsistency as it pertains to the mandated application period. This falls under the definition of Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction.
